elements.sent-2411-001: Lattice $\zz$ theories with complex actions share many key features with finite-density QCD including a sign problem and $\cCK$ symmetry.
elements.sent-2411-002: Complex $\zz$ spin and gauge models exhibit a generalized Kramers-Wannier duality mapping them onto chiral $\zz$ spin and gauge models, which are simulatable with standard lattice methods in large regions of parameter space.
elements.sent-2411-003: The Migdal-Kadanoff real-space renormalization group (RG) preserves this duality, and we use it to compute the approximate phase diagram of both spin and gauge $\zz$ models in dimensions one through four.
elements.sent-2411-004: Chiral $\zz$ spin models are known to exhibit a Devil's Flower phase structure, with inhomogeneous phases that can be thought of as $\zz$ analogues of chiral spirals.
elements.sent-2411-005: Out of the large class of models we study, we find that only chiral spin models and their duals have a Devil's Flower structure with an infinite set of inhomogeneous phases, a result we attribute to Elitzur's theorem.
elements.sent-2411-006: We also find that different forms of the Migdal-Kadanoff RG produce different numbers of phases, a violation of the expectation for universal behavior from a real-space RG.
elements.sent-2411-007: We discuss extensions of our work to $\zn$ models, SU($N$) models and nonzero temperature.
elements.sent-2411-008: A central objective of nuclear physics is mapping out the phases of QCD in the baryon density-temperature ($\mub$-$T$) plane \cite{796947, Akiba:2015jwa, Achenbach:2023pba .}
elements.sent-2411-009: Several major collider experiments are pursuing this goal, including RHIC at Brookhaven \cite{STAR:2002eio, PHENIX:2015siv , ALICE at CERN \cite{ALICE:2008ngc , and the planned CBM experiment at FAIR \cite{CBM:2016kpk .}}}
elements.sent-2411-010: To complement this work, a simultaneous theoretical effort is targeting QCD phase structure from first principles \cite{Busza:2018rrf ; however, at present we lack rigorous and systematically improvable methods to probe much of $\mub$-$T$ plane.}
elements.sent-2411-011: On the numerical front, lattice QCD calculations at $\mub \gtrsim T$ are obstructed by a sign problem \cite{Aarts:2015tyj,Nagata:2021ugx, Ratti:2018ksb, Ratti:2021ubw .}
elements.sent-2411-012: While many approaches are under development to overcome sign problems \cite{Barbour:1997ej, Fodor:2001au, Fodor:2001pe, Fodor:2007vv, Alexandru:2014hga,Alexandru:2005ix, Kratochvila:2005mk, Ejiri:2008xt,Gattringer:2014nxa,Cristoforetti:2012su, Alexandru:2015xva, Alexandru:2015sua,Parisi:1983mgm, Attanasio:2020spv,Albergo:2019eim, Lawrence:2021izu,Rico:2013qya, Pichler:2015yqa, Kadoh:2018hqq, Kadoh:2018tis, Kuramashi:2018mmi, Magnifico:2020bqt, Yuan:2020xmq, Meurice:2022xbk , they cannot fully handle QCD yet.}
elements.sent-2411-013: On the analytic front, various effective field theories (EFTs) have been developed such as hard thermal loops \cite{Braaten:1989mz, Andersen:2004fp and high density effective theory \cite{Hong:1999ru, Schafer:2003jn , but these only cover limited regions of parameter space.}}
elements.sent-2411-014: In the near term, there exist few viable first-principles approaches to finite-density QCD, such as Taylor expansions off the $T$-axis \cite{Allton:2002zi, Allton:2005gk, Gavai:2008zr, Kaczmarek:2011zz and studies at imaginary baryon chemical potential \cite{Dagotto:1989fw, Hasenfratz:1991ax, Alford:1998sd, deForcrand:2002hgr, DElia:2002tig .}}
elements.sent-2411-015: A more recently proposed approach to understanding finite-density QCD capitalizes on one of its symmetries, combined charge and complex conjugation symmetry $\cCK$ \cite{Meisinger:2012va, Schindler:2019ugo, Schindler:2021otf .}
elements.sent-2411-016: We can see this symmetry directly from the QCD Lagrangian at finite density \cite{Schindler:2021otf , \begin{equation}\label{eq:euclidean-qcd} \cL_{\rm QCD}^{\rm Eucl} = \bar\psi_j (i\slashed{D}-m)\psi_j+\frac{1}{4}G_{\mu\nu}^AG_{\mu\nu A} + i\mub \bar\psi \gamma^4 \psi\,,\end{equation} where $\psi_j$ are fermion fields, $D$ is a covariant derivative, $G$ is the gluon field strength, $\mub$ is the baryon chemical potential, and $\gamma^i$ represents the standard Dirac matrices.}
elements.sent-2411-017: It is well known that when $\mub=0$, QCD preserves charge conjugation $\cC$, parity inversion $\cP$, and time-reversal $\cT$ symmetries individually and in combination.
elements.sent-2411-018: The situation changes substantially when we turn on a nonzero baryon density term $\mub$ in \eq{euclidean-qcd .}
elements.sent-2411-019: While the fermion bilinear $\bar\psi \gamma^4 \psi$ remains invariant under $\cP$ and $\cT$, it flips sign under $\cC$.
elements.sent-2411-020: In turn, the lattice path integral weights in \eq{euclidean-qcd and the fermion determinant become complex, the transfer matrix becomes non-Hermitian, and QCD develops a sign problem.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Additionally, \eq{euclidean-qcd loses Lorentz invariance at $\mub \neq 0$, as the chemical potential picks out a preferred direction ($\gamma^4$ rather than $\gamma^\mu$).}
elements.sent-2411-022: However, the complex conjugation operation $\cK$ flips the sign of $i$ while leaving the constant $\mub$ and the bilinear $\psibar\gamma^4\psi$ invariant.
elements.sent-2411-023: Thus, the combined operation $\cCK$ leaves the entire Lagrangian invariant.
elements.sent-2411-024: The operation $\cCK$ belongs to the class of $\cPT$-type symmetries, which are widely studied in optics and condensed matter for their unique properties and extensive experimental applications \cite{Bender:1998ke,Bender:2019cwm,Bender:2023cem,RevModPhys.88.035002, Feng2017, Ozdemir2019, Miri2019, Ashida:2020dkc, Ding:2022juv .}
elements.sent-2411-025: A $\cPT$-type symmetry is any symmetry combining one linear operator (e.g. $\cP$ or $\cC$) and one antilinear operator (e.g. $\cT$ or $\cK$).
elements.sent-2411-026: Importantly, $\cPT$-symmetric matrices may be non-Hermitian; however, every eigenvalue of a $\cPT$-symmetric system is always either real or part of a complex-conjugate pair \cite{Bender:1998ke, Bender:2007nj .}
elements.sent-2411-027: The study of quantum field theories with non-Hermitian transfer matrices and $\cPT$-type symmetries is a far younger field than $\cPT$-symmetric quantum mechanics and optics.
elements.sent-2411-028: Nonetheless, many steps have been taken towards developing a formal understanding of and techniques for these systems \cite{Bender:2003ve, Bender:2004sv, Bender:2007wu,Ivanov:2007me, Curtright:2006aq, Curtright:2007wh, Ai:2022csx, Lawrence:2023woz, Romatschke:2022llf, Bender:2018pbv, Bender:2022eze, Alexandre:2017foi, Felski:2021evi, Bender:2021fxa, Fring:2020wrj, Fring:2021zci, Correa:2021pwi, Chernodub:2017lmx .}
elements.sent-2411-029: A number of $\cPT$-QFTs have been studied within the context of Beyond the Standard Model (BSM) model building \cite{Jones-Smith:2009qeu, Ohlsson:2015xsa, Chen:2024bya, Alexandre:2015kra, Alexandre:2020tba, Mavromatos:2020hfy, Alexandre:2020bet, Mavromatos:2021hpe, Mavromatos:2022heh, Ogilvie:2021wvb, Mannheim:2011ds .}
elements.sent-2411-030: Within the Standard Model, several models sharing features of dense QCD have been analyzed using tools based on non-Hermiticity and $\cCK$ symmetry \cite{Meisinger:2012va, Nishimura:2014rxa, Nishimura:2014kla, Nishimura:2015lit, Nishimura:2016yue, Schindler:2021cke,Meisinger:2013zba .}
elements.sent-2411-031: Most importantly for this work, theories with non-Hermitian transfer matrices and $\cPT$-type symmetries generally support unusual phase structure not seen in conventional field theories \cite{Schindler:2019ugo, Schindler:2021cke, Schindler:2021otf, Ogilvie:2021wvb .}
elements.sent-2411-032: The appearance of complex conjugate pairs of transfer matrix eigenvalues is a hallmark of $\cPT$-type symmetries, leading to sinusoidally-modulated exponential decay in correlation functions, similar to Friedel oscillations \cite{fetter2012quantum, Kapusta:1988fi .}
elements.sent-2411-033: Regions of parameter space with conjugate pairs may also behave as moat regimes \cite{Pisarski:2021qof , a name that originates in the condensed matter literature \cite{Pu2024,Sedrakyan14 .}}
elements.sent-2411-034: The boundary between a region where all eigenvalues are positive and a region with complex conjugate pairs is referred to in the statistical mechanics literature as a disorder line \cite{stephenson_ising_1970 .}
elements.sent-2411-035: We summarize the possible behaviors in \tab{mass-matrix .}
elements.sent-2411-036: Note that an arbitrary non-Hermitian system without a $\cPT$-type symmetry generally has a mix of positive, negative, and complex eigenvalues and thus does not in general support the types of stable exotic phases we see in $\cPT$ systems.

elements.sent-2411-041: Note that one well-studied class of inhomogeneous field configurations that will relate to inhomogeneous phases in this work are chiral spirals, nonlinear waves with expected values of $\sigma$ and $\pi_3$ behaving as a spira; i.e., $\left< \sigma+i\pi_3\right> \sim \exp(ik\cdot r)$ \cite{Schon:2000he, Kojo:2009ha .}
elements.sent-2411-042: The widespread presence of exotic phases in finite-density models suggests that QCD phase structure could be more complicated than conventionally anticipated, particularly near the QCD phase transition and hypothesized critical endpoint \cite{Nussinov:2024erh .}
elements.sent-2411-043: Experimental signatures of moat regimes in heavy ion collisions have been developed, including in Hanbury-Brown-Twiss (HBT) interferometry \cite{Pisarski:2020gkx, Pisarski:2021qof, Rennecke:2023xhc, Fukushima:2023tpv and enhanced dilepton production \cite{Nussinov:2024erh .}}
elements.sent-2411-044: In this work, we compute the phase diagram of lattice spin and gauge models with $\zz$ symmetry (the center of the QCD gauge group SU(3)) and $\cCK$-symmetric complex couplings, which mimic a chemical potential and induce a sign problem.
elements.sent-2411-045: Due to $\cCK$ symmetry, we can often construct dualities between complex $\zz$ models and sign problem-free chiral $\zz$ models.
elements.sent-2411-046: The duality is a complex-chiral generalization of the Kramers-Wannier duality of the Ising model.
elements.sent-2411-047: Next, we generalize the real-space renormalization group (RG) for use in systems with chemical potential, and we use it to compute the phase diagram of the complex and chiral $\zz$ models.
elements.sent-2411-048: One class of these models, chiral $\zz$ spin systems, exhibit $\zz$ analogues of chiral spirals: an imaginary analogue of chemical potential induces a phase in which $\zz$ variables exhibit sinusoidal modulation along a given direction.
elements.sent-2411-049: We predict the phase diagrams for broad classes of lattice $\zz$ spin and gauge models with real and imaginary chemical potentials for in three and four dimensions, determining which have $\zz$ spirals.
elements.sent-2411-050: We also explore the extension of our results to $\zn$ models with $N > 3$ and to SU($N$) models with $N\ge 3$.
elements.sent-2411-051: The outline of this paper is as follows.
elements.sent-2411-052: In the remainder of \sec{intro , we introduce the $\zz$ models that form the focus of our paper.}
elements.sent-2411-053: In \sec{techniques , we introduce two key techniques used in this paper, Kramers-Wannier duality and the Migdal-Kadanoff renormalization group (RG), and discuss their extensions to complex and chiral models.}
elements.sent-2411-054: In \sec{results , we use the Migdal-Kadanoff RG to compute the phase diagram of our models.}
elements.sent-2411-055: In \sec{pt-z3-discussion , we discuss our results, highlighting how different RG schemes lead to qualitatively different phase diagrams in finite-density models.}
elements.sent-2411-056: We also extend our results to certain SU($N$) lattice models.
elements.sent-2411-057: Finally, in \sec{outlook , we offer concluding remarks and outlook.}
elements.sent-2411-058: We define 1D complex and chiral $\zz$ spin models with actions \begin{equation}\label{eq:models} S_{c}[s_{j}] =\sum_{j=1}^{M}\left[\frac{J}{2}\left(s_{j}s_{j+1}^{*}+s_{j}^* s_{j+1}\right)+\frac{\theta}{\sqrt{3}}\left(s_{j}s_{j+1}^{*}-s_{j}^* s_{j+1}\right)\right] \nonumber S_{\chi}[s_{j}] =\sum_{j=1}^{M}\frac{\tilde J}{2}\left(e^{i\tilde\theta}s_{j}s_{j+1}^{*}+e^{-i\tilde\theta}s_{j}^ *s_{j+1}\right)\,,\end{equation} respectively.
elements.sent-2411-059: Here, $s_j$ are $\zn$ spin variables taking values $\exp[2\pi n/N]$, the parameter $j$ runs over the $M$ lattice sites, and the parameters $(J,\theta)$ and $(\tilde J, \tilde \theta)$ are real-valued.
elements.sent-2411-060: In $S_c$, the complex interaction term $\theta$ induces a nonzero density.
elements.sent-2411-061: The parametrization we use for $S_c$ is not the standard one, which can be obtained from $S_\chi$ via the analytic continuation $\tilde\theta \to -i\mu$.
elements.sent-2411-062: It is easy to see that $\mu \sim \theta$ for small $\mu$, and the general relation between parametrizations is straightforward.
elements.sent-2411-063: However, the $(J,\theta)$ parametrization is more convenient for RG calculations.
elements.sent-2411-064: To define higher-dimensional complex (chiral) spin models, we impose the interaction with $\theta\neq 0$ ($\tilde\theta\neq 0$) in only a single direction, leaving the other $d-1$ transverse directions as standard-nearest neighbor interactions with couplings $J$ ($\tilde J$).
elements.sent-2411-065: In all dimensions, the complex spin models are a model of $\zn$ particles in the presence of a chemical potential.
elements.sent-2411-066: To define higher-dimensional gauge models, we replace site-based spins with link-based gauge fields, and we replace nearest-neighbor spin interactions with Wilson plaquette interactions.
elements.sent-2411-067: Just as in the spin models, we take the complex (chiral) term $\theta$ ($\tilde\theta$) to be nonzero in only a single preferred plane.
elements.sent-2411-068: In the complex gauge models, the $\theta$ term corresponds to a background electric field that is real in Minkowski space and imaginary in Euclidean space; this field induces a sign problem on the lattice.
elements.sent-2411-069: The $\tilde\theta$ interaction in the chiral gauge models gives rise to a real magnetic field in both Euclidean and Minkowski space.
elements.paragraph-2411-001: Phase structure of real and chiral $\zn$ models.
elements.sent-2411-070: The phase structure of standard $\zn$ models (where $\theta$ and $\tilde \theta$ are both zero) has been extensively investigated \cite{Banks:1977cc, PhysRevD.19.3698, PhysRevD.19.3715, PhysRevB.16.1217, Ukawa:1979yv, Cardy:1981fd .}
elements.sent-2411-071: Less is known about general chiral and complex $\zn$ models, though many studies of chiral spin models have been carried out.
elements.sent-2411-072: In three and higher dimensions, chiral $\zz$ spin models have an infinite number of stable inhomogeneous phases that are commensurate with the underlying lattice in the low temperature (small $\tilde J$) region.
elements.sent-2411-073: In a given inhomogeneous phase, $(d-1)$-dimensional sheets of spins, each characterized by a certain expectation value, are layered along the chiral direction \cite{PhysRevB.24.398,Yeomans1981, PhysRevB.24.5180, selke_monte_1982, Yeomans1982, Bak1982, mccullough_mean-field_1992,Asorey:1993ya .}
elements.sent-2411-074: These phases are $\zz$ analogues of chiral spirals, with each phase corresponding to a particular wave number.
elements.sent-2411-076: It is likely that the $N\to\infty$ limit is smooth, although to our knowledge this has not been investigated.
elements.sent-2411-077: The phase diagrams of the chiral spin models exhibit a fractal structure called a Devil's flower, by analogy with the well-known Devil's staircase \cite{10.1007/BF02418423 .}
elements.sent-2411-078: The behavior of $\zz$ spin models in 2D is a special case, and is closely tied to the physics of the BKT transition \cite{Berezinsky:1970fr, Kosterlitz:1973xp .}
elements.sent-2411-079: For example, there is a nontrivial critical point on the positive real axis associated with a second-order phase transition, whereas higher dimensions have first-order transitions.
elements.sent-2411-080: It is known that a technique called the Migdal-Kadanoff real-space RG (which we discuss below in detail) does only a fair job of capturing 2D $\zn$ critical behavior \cite{Jose:1977gm .}
elements.sent-2411-081: Next, we introduce two key techniques that we will use in our analysis: Kramers-Wannier duality in \secs{kw-real {kw-complex , and the real-space RG in \secs{mkrg-real {mkrg-complex .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-082: Kramers-Wannier duality maps the 2D Ising model onto itself, interchanging low- and high-temperature behavior \cite{Kramers:1941kn, Savit:1979ny .}
elements.sent-2411-083: It is simple to establish this duality by mapping the Boltzmann weights of one model to the character expansion of the other \cite{Ukawa:1979yv .\footnote{The weights of a path integral are simply the exponential of the discretized action $w(\vec{x ) = \exp[-S(\vec{x )]$.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-084: A character expansion is simply an expansion of a function on some group $\cO = \sum_i c_i \chi_i$ as a linear combination of the characters $\chi_i$ of the group's irreducible representations.
elements.sent-2411-085: This is simple to see in the 2D Ising model, defined as \begin{equation}\cH = -J \sum_{j,k} \sigma_j \sigma_k\,,\end{equation} where $J$ and $h$ are couplings, $\{j,k\}$ are nearest-neighbor sites, and $\sigma_i$ are $\bZ_2$ spin variables.
elements.sent-2411-086: Using shorthand notation to write nearest-neighbor interactions $\sigma_j \sigma_k = \sigma_\ell$, we can write the character expansion of each Ising model Boltzmann weight as \begin{equation}w(\sigma_\ell) = e^{K \sigma} = \cosh K + \sigma_\ell \sinh K \,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-087: The Boltzmann weights themselves are $\exp(\tilde K\sigma_0) = \{\exp(\tilde K), \exp(-\tilde K)\}$.
elements.sent-2411-088: We only care about the relative weighting of the Boltzmann weights, so we can construct a duality \begin{equation}\exp(-2\tilde K) \leftrightarrow \tanh K\,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-089: This duality maps the small-$K$ region of the Ising model, which is paramagnetic with zero magnetization, to the large-$K$ region of the Ising model, which is ferromagnetic with nonzero magnetization.
elements.sent-2411-090: The fixed point of the duality transform is the critical point of the Ising model.
elements.sent-2411-091: This duality extends to operators \cite{Kadanoff:1970kz , and is the most well-known example of order-disorder duality.}
elements.sent-2411-092: This duality extends to other $\bZ_2$ and $\zn$ models generally.
elements.sent-2411-093: The form of the duality is dimension-dependent, as shown in \tab{dualities .}
elements.sent-2411-094: In spin systems, nearest-neighbor spins are connected by links.
elements.sent-2411-095: The Kramers-Wannier dual of a link interaction on a square lattice in two dimensions is a link on the dual lattice perpendicular to the original link.
elements.sent-2411-096: In three dimensions, the dual of a link is a plaquette in 3D, and a cube in 4D.
elements.sent-2411-097: Thus, the dual of a $\zn$ spin system is a $\zn$ spin system in two dimensions, a gauge theory in three dimensions, and a theory of fundamental plaquettes interacting around a cube in four dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-098: Similarly, the dual of a $\zn$ gauge theory is a $\zn$ spin theory in three dimensions and a $\zn$ gauge theory in four dimension.
elements.sent-2411-099: For a concise treatment of Abelian lattice duality, see e.g. \cite{Ukawa:1979yv .}
elements.sent-2411-100: Building off studies of similar $\cPT$ $\zn$ models in \refscite{Meisinger:2012va, Meisinger:2013zba and studies of duality in other $\cPT$-symmetric lattice models \cite{Ogilvie:2018fov , it is straightforward to show that complex and chiral $\zn$ models exhibit an extension of the Kramers-Wannier duality.}}
elements.sent-2411-101: We begin by examining the character expansion and Boltzmann weights of our chiral and complex models, which allows us to demonstrate a duality between these models.
elements.sent-2411-102: Next, we highlight the regions of parameter space in which the complex models have a sign problem-free dual form.
elements.sent-2411-103: We can write the action for a generic $\zz$-invariant nearest-neighbor interaction between a single pair of spins $s_j$ and $s_k$ connected by a link $s$ as: \begin{equation}\label{eq:link-action} A(s)=a+bs+cs^*\,,\end{equation} with $s = s_j s_k^*$ for nearest neighbors $j=k\pm 1$ and $a,b,c \in \mathbb C$.
elements.sent-2411-104: For a conventional $\zn$ model, the link action must be real, implying invariance under complex conjugation $\cK$.
elements.sent-2411-105: We also require invariance under charge conjugation $\cC$, which takes $s \to s^*$.
elements.sent-2411-106: These two conditions fix \eq{link-action to \begin{equation}A_{\rm H}(s)=a+b(s+s^*)\,,\end{equation} where $a,b\in \mathbb R$.}
elements.sent-2411-107: This link action gives rise to a positive Boltzmann weight for $b>-a/2$.
elements.sent-2411-108: To formulate the chiral $\zz$ nearest-neighbor action, we remove the condition that \eq{link-action is invariant under $\cC$ and only require the action to be real, finding that \begin{equation}\label{eq:chiral-link-action} A_{\chi}(s)=a+bs+b^*s^*\,,\end{equation} where $a\in\mathbb R$ and $b\in \mathbb C$.}
elements.sent-2411-109: The complex $\zz$ action requires invariance under combined $\cCK$ without imposing invariance under $\cC$ or $\cK$ individually.
elements.sent-2411-111: As we are working with $\zz$ variables, we can express the symmetry of the action as \begin{equation}\label{eq:complex-link-action} A_c(q)=A_c^*(-q)\,,\end{equation} where $s = \exp[2\pi i q/3]$.
elements.sent-2411-112: This bears close resemblance to the $\cCK$ symmetry condition in quantum mechanics, $V(x) = V^*(-x)$ \cite{Bender:1998ke .}
elements.sent-2411-113: Under these conditions, $\{a,b,c\} \in \bR$ in \eq{link-action , but $A_c(s)$ is complex as the $\zz$ spin variables are complex.}
elements.sent-2411-114: The symmetries of a link action $A(s)$ typically are inherited by their corresponding Boltzmann weights $w(s) = \exp A(s)$.
elements.sent-2411-115: Note that we carried out this analysis explicitly only for the spin models, but the same procedure carries over straightforwardly to our gauge models, where the action is a sum over plaquette terms.
elements.sent-2411-116: The character expansion of the Boltzmann weights in the chiral $\zz$ model is \begin{equation}\label{eq:chiral-boltzmann-weights} w_\chi(s)=1+zs+z^* s^*\end{equation} where $s\in \zz$ is a link variable between nearest-neighbors $s_js^*_k$.
elements.sent-2411-117: The parameter $z$ is complex if the link lies along the chiral direction, and is real otherwise.
elements.sent-2411-118: In the complex model, we have \begin{equation}w_c(s)=\exp\left[{J\over 2}\left(s+s^*\right)+{\theta\over \sqrt 3}\left(s-s^*\right)\right]\,.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-119: This implies that the complex model has weights $e^J$ and $e^{-J/2 \pm i\theta}$.
elements.sent-2411-120: Now that we have the character expansion and Boltzmann weights of the complex and chiral models, we can form a duality between them.
elements.sent-2411-121: Like conventional Kramers-Wannier duality, the nature of the complex-chiral duality is dimension dependent, as summarized in \tab{dualities .}
elements.sent-2411-122: As before, we only care about relative weights, so we can find a duality relation for complex and chiral models by equating the ratio of character expansion coefficients of chiral models and the ratio of Boltzmann weights for complex models.

elements.sent-2411-136: The Migdal-Kadanoff renormalization group (MKRG) yields qualitative information about the phase structure of lattice systems \cite{Migdal:1975zf, Migdal:1975zg, PhysRevLett.34.1005, Kadanoff1976 .}
elements.sent-2411-137: Due to its simplicity and utility, this technique has been extensively studied and applied to many models \cite{RevModPhys.49.267 .}
elements.sent-2411-138: Effectively, we apply a Migdal-Kadanoff RG transform $R$ multiple times to a system, and map out the basins of attraction of the model, which tell us the phase structure of a system.
elements.sent-2411-139: The MKRG is approximate on conventional lattices, and exact on hierarchical lattices \cite{derrida_fractal_1983 .}
elements.sent-2411-140: Interestingly, MKRG schemes respect Kramers-Wannier dualities \cite{Kadanoff1976 , and thus give us information about both a given $\zn$ model and its dual.}
elements.sent-2411-141: The MKRG is built from two primary operations: decimation ($D_\lambda$) and bond-moving ($B_\lambda$), as shown in \fig{rg-transforms .}
elements.sent-2411-142: A decimation transformation of a 1D spin lattice changes the lattice spacing from $a$ to $\lambda a$ by integrating out every $(\lambda-1)$ out of $\lambda$ spins, leaving us with lattice points spaced by $\lambda a$ from one another.
elements.sent-2411-143: In a 1D Ising model characterized by nearest-neighbor couplings $J$ and spacing $a$, decimation creates a new Ising model with coupling $J^\prime$ and lattice spacing $\lambda a$.
elements.sent-2411-144: In the 1D Ising model, decimation acts as $\tanh J^\prime = \tanh^\lambda(J)$.

elements.sent-2411-146: Bond moving is a technique which changes local interactions in a way which allows decimation to be carried out in higher dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-147: A bond-moving transformation on a spin system moves all the bonds inside a $d$-dimensional hypercube of size $(\lambda a)^d$ to links on the boundaries of the hypercube, changing the strength of an interaction on the boundary of the hypercube from $J$ to $\lambda^{d-1 J$.}
elements.sent-2411-148: Let us use lowercase $b_\lambda$ and $d_\lambda$ to represent MKRG operations in $\zz$ models.

elements.sent-2411-150: Every MKRG transformation for $\zz$ lattice models can be written as the composition of a sequence of these two operations.

elements.sent-2411-325: In this work, we studied lattice $\zz$ spin and gauge theories with complex and chiral interactions.
elements.sent-2411-326: We demonstrated an extension of Kramers-Wannier duality that maps complex and chiral models onto one another.
elements.sent-2411-327: We analyzed the phase structure of these models with real-space RG methods, and showed how $\zz$ and $\cCK$ symmetries impact the RG flow and phase structure.
elements.sent-2411-328: In particular, we showed that spatially-modulated phases appear in both chiral and complex models, and that these phases manifest in a Devil's flower structure in the specific case of chiral spin models and their duals.
elements.sent-2411-329: We have shown that the phase structure of $\zz$ non-Hermitian models obtained from Migdal-Kadanoff RG calculations depends strongly on the order of bond moving and decimation, a violation of expectations of universality.
elements.sent-2411-330: This finding underscores the need to explore whether other real-space RG formulations, and other theoretical tools like mean field theory exhibit similar issues when applied to non-Hermitian and finite-density models.
elements.sent-2411-331: In cases where a real dual form is available, standard lattice methods can in principle determine the correct phase structure.
elements.sent-2411-332: In practice, it may be necessary to consider both order and disorder variables for a complete understanding.
elements.sent-2411-333: We have also found that the Devil's flower phase structure only occurs in a given model if there is a gauge-invariant order parameter available in the model or its dual, a behavior we trace to Elitzur's theorem.
elements.sent-2411-334: This has profound implications for the appearance of inhomogeneous phases in lattice field theories in general.
elements.sent-2411-335: We have partially extended our results on $\zz$ to $\zn$ and SU($N$) lattice models.
elements.sent-2411-336: The $\zn$ chiral spin models with $N\ge 3$ are all expected to have $\zn$ spirals.
elements.sent-2411-337: As in the $\zz$ case, we predict that chiral SU($N$) spin models exhibit a Devil's flower phase structure for $d\geq3$, but that chiral gauge theories do not, due to Elitzur's theorem.
elements.sent-2411-338: Unfortunately, the lack of a simple duality for non-Abelian lattice models prevents us from making corresponding statements about complex SU($N$) lattice models, which are more directly related to finite-density QCD.
elements.sent-2411-339: It is possible that chiral spirals and $\zn$ spirals may have a synergistic effect for models in which Polyakov loops and fermion bilinear order parameters are coupled, such as PNJL models.
